title: What Causes Hives?
description:
What causes hives? When your body is exposed to something, either through breathing it in or eating or drinking it, or having it touch your skin, there are cells called mast cells. These cells release histamine, and that histamine causes your skin to cause hives, essentially welts. And what happens is histamine causes your blood vessels to go from being small to being big, in that sense, blood to the area, and cause the swelling.

Now, if the hives are really bad, you can try something like Zyrtec, Claritin, or antihistamines that can make the hives go down. If the hives are also associated with throat closing or symptoms that are scary, you may want to take what's called an EpiPen. And if they keep happening, you might want to go see an allergist or get an allergy test.

title: Hives: What Happens Inside Your Body 😮 #shorts #urticaria - Creativelearning3d
description:
Ever had those red, itchy bumps suddenly show up on your skin? Those are hives! When your body reacts to something like stress or allergies, it releases histamine from your cells. This causes your blood vessels to expand and leak fluid, which creates the swelling under your skin. Your nerves also kick in, sending signals to your brain that make you feel itchy.

Hives can be triggered by different things—food, stress, or even the weather. The good news? They usually disappear on their own!

title: Urticaria (Hives or Welts) Causes, Signs, Diagnosis and Treatment
description:
Urticaria, commonly known as hives, is a skin condition characterized by the sudden appearance of itchy, raised welts or red, swollen bumps on the skin. It can be a distressing and uncomfortable condition, but with proper understanding, diagnosis, and treatment, individuals suffering from urticaria can manage their symptoms effectively. In this article, we will delve into the various aspects of urticaria, including its causes, symptoms, diagnosis, and treatment options.

I. Types of Urticaria

Acute Urticaria:

Lasts for a short duration, typically less than six weeks.
Often caused by allergies, insect stings, medications, or infections.
Can be triggered by specific factors and usually resolves on its own.
Chronic Urticaria:

Persists for more than six weeks and can last for months or even years.
The exact cause is often more challenging to determine than acute urticaria.
May be related to autoimmune disorders or other underlying medical conditions.

II. Causes of Urticaria

Allergic Reactions:

Common allergens include certain foods (e.g., nuts, shellfish, dairy), medications (e.g., antibiotics, aspirin), and insect stings or bites.
The body's immune response to these allergens triggers the release of histamine, leading to hives.

Non-Allergic Triggers:

Physical triggers such as cold or heat, pressure, vibration, and sunlight can induce urticaria in some individuals (known as physical urticaria).
Stress, infections, and hormonal changes can also contribute to the development of hives.
Autoimmune Disorders:

Some cases of chronic urticaria are associated with autoimmune diseases like lupus or thyroid disorders.
In these cases, the immune system mistakenly targets the body's own tissues, leading to hives.

III. Symptoms of Urticaria

Welts or Hives: Raised, red or pink bumps on the skin.
Vary in size and shape and can appear anywhere on the body.
Often surrounded by an area of redness and itchiness.

Itchiness: One of the hallmark symptoms of urticaria.
Intense itching is common and can significantly affect a person's quality of life.

Swelling: Hives can cause localized or widespread swelling, known as angioedema.
Swelling often affects the lips, eyelids, hands, feet, and genitals.

Fluctuating Symptoms: Urticaria symptoms can come and go quickly, often within a few hours.
New hives may appear as others resolve, creating a constantly changing pattern.

IV. Diagnosis

If you suspect you have urticaria, it is crucial to consult a healthcare professional for an accurate diagnosis. Diagnosis typically involves:

Medical History:

Discussing your symptoms, their duration, and any potential triggers or underlying medical conditions.
Physical Examination:

The doctor will examine your skin and may inquire about your recent activities or exposures.
Allergy Testing:

To identify potential allergens, skin or blood tests may be conducted.

Other Tests:

In cases of chronic urticaria, further tests may be required to rule out underlying conditions, such as autoimmune disorders.

V. Treatment Options

Antihistamines: First-line treatment for urticaria.
Over-the-counter or prescription antihistamines can help alleviate itching and reduce the appearance of hives.

Avoidance of Triggers: Identifying and avoiding specific triggers, such as allergens or physical factors, can prevent recurrent episodes.

Corticosteroids: Short-term use of oral or topical corticosteroids may be recommended for severe acute urticaria.

Immune Modulators: In cases of chronic urticaria, medications that modulate the immune system, like omalizumab, may be prescribed.

Lifestyle Modifications: Stress management techniques and lifestyle adjustments can help reduce the frequency and severity of hives.


Conclusion

Urticaria is a common skin condition that can be caused by various factors, including allergies, physical triggers, and underlying medical conditions. While it can be uncomfortable and disruptive, with the right diagnosis and treatment, individuals with urticaria can effectively manage their symptoms. If you suspect you have urticaria, seek medical advice to determine the cause and receive appropriate treatment to regain control of your skin health.

title: Foods To Avoid in Urticaria
description:
Foods to Avoid in Urticaria -

🚫 If you have urticaria (hives), certain foods can trigger flare-ups! Here are some common culprits:
❌ Besan (Gram Flour) – Can cause allergic reactions in some individuals.
❌ Eggplant (Brinjal) – Contains histamines that may worsen symptoms.
❌ Khatai (Sour Foods) – Citrus fruits, tamarind, and pickles can aggravate hives.
❌ Fish – High in histamines, a common trigger for urticaria.
❌ Chinese Food – Often contains MSG and additives that may cause allergic reactions.
❌ Tomato – Rich in histamines, which can worsen skin irritation.
❌ Potato – Some people react to solanine, found in potatoes.
❌ Red & Orange Flavored Foods – Artificial colors and preservatives can be problematic.

description:

Hi guys, welcome back to Sg Dr wellness. I am Dr Chen Yiming,
Today I will be sharing about a very very itchy and irritating condition called urticaria or hives.
Most of you must have had this before.
Very itchy bumps on the skin that comes and go and looks like mosquito bites.
What are these caused by?
What is the treatment ?
What are the red flags to look out for?
Watch till the end.
And if you are new to my channel, click on the subscribe button now.

What is urticaria or hives.
These are itchy raised bumps that can occur on the skin.
They can look like individual small bumps like these or may involve large patches of our skin.
They are warm and hot and extremely itchy.
It may be associated sometimes with swelling of the eye and lips in what is known as angio-oedema. 

On a microscopic level, what happened is that
- there is a sudden release of large amounts of histamine from our immune cells
- and that results in a sudden enlargement of small blood vessels within our skin 
- followed by a rush of fluid out of our vessels into our skin causing an itchy swelling.

Urticaria is very common.
About 20% of us would have experienced at least 1 episode of this at any time of our lives.

If these hives are short-lived, they are termed acute urticaria 
If they last for more than 6 weeks, then they are called chronic urticaria.

Today I will focus more on acute urticarias, which are hives that generally last for days to weeks.
In my practice, this is commonly seen in children.

Some of the common causes of acute hives are
1) fever and viral illnesses
2) reaction to allergens like dust or latex
3) parasitic infections like mites or scabies or insect bites
4) physical stimulations like sun exposure, or change in temperature from hot to cold or vice versa
5) less commonly drug or food allergy 
In 50% of the time, we may not be able to identify any cause and that can be very frustrating for the child and parents

The hives come and generally disappears within 24 hours but another batch of hives may appear in the same area or a different area.
This process may repeat itself for many days or weeks.
It is so itchy that it may affect sleep and lessons or work.

Are treatments available for hives?
Yes of course.
Antihistamines are the main treatment of choice
Some of the drowsy ones are piriton, diphenhydramine, hydroxyzine or promethazine
The non drowsy ones includes Zyrtec, loratadine, telfast, aerius
In some instances, we may even add in a different class of antihistamines like ranitidine or cimetidine.

Now if we are able to identify any triggers, the next course of action would be to treat the triggers or to avoid the triggers.
Treat the viral infections, or avoid that particular drug that may trigger the hives.
This will possibly prevent the same thing from happening again.

If you have had hives before, keep this medications in your medicine cabinet because hives tend to recur.

Now, are there any signs which indicate that these hives are due to the more sinister causes?
If you notice that each individual spot lasts more than 24 hours OR
They cause bleeding under the skin OR
After the hives disappear you notice a brownish pigmentation that is left behind?
Then you may need to see a dermatologist for a skin biopsy to exclude the possibility of a vasculitis. 

Otherwise in the large majority of cases, they are harmless. 

Thank you for watching till the end.
Subscribe and like my channel. 
Hope this video helps in the treatment of your urticaria.
Treatment is straightforward and some of the medications are available over the counter.
Till the next time, stay safe and stay healthy.

title: Do's and Don'ts of Uticaria (Hives)
description:
Hives or Utiricia are swollen, small, pale red bumps or larger patches that suddenly appear on the skin with severe itching usually. Hives can be allergic or non allergic. Allergic hives are triggered by an overreaction of the immune system to certain things, most commonly foods (nuts, shellfish), insect stings and bites.

Symptoms include itching, or less often burning or stinging; raised, often red, swellings; and separate swellings that usually go away in 24 hours, but new ones often appear as the old ones disappear.

DO's
✔️ Soothe your Skin by taking cool showers or applying a cool pack.
✔️ One of the triggers is Stress. Practice Meditations or take deep breaths to control Stress.
✔️ Avoid consuming Milk and Eggs since they can trigger Hives.
✔️ Protect yourself against insect bites.
✔️ Avoid touching pollens.

DON'TS
✖️ Rubbing your Skin vigorously or using the towel after shower.
✖️ Scratching your skin because this can make the hives worse.
✖️ Consuming Allergic Food.
✖️ Exposing yourself to sun as it can trigger your Hives.
✖️ Consuming Peanuts and Shellfish as they are most common allergic food.

In case of difficulty in breathing or swallowing, seek medical attention immediately.

title: Chronic urticaria- homoeopathy medicine . #natural  #homoeopathytreatment #chronic #urticaria
Allergic To Water?! Aquagenic Urticaria
description:
It’s rare—but it’s real. A condition called Aquagenic Urticaria causes people to break out in painful hives whenever their skin touches water. That’s right—even sweat, tears, or a shower can trigger burning, itching, and welts! Only about 100 cases have ever been reported worldwide. What causes it? Doctors aren’t exactly sure—but some think the water interacts with elements in the skin to release histamines, causing an allergic-like reaction. There’s no cure, and even drinking water can be painful for some. It’s one of the most bizarre and frustrating conditions out there—and a powerful reminder that for some, even the most essential substance on Earth can become the enemy.

Recorded, Filmed and Edited By: Keira Boland

title: Surprising Triggers for Chronic Hives
description:
When hives don’t go away after 6+ weeks, the triggers aren’t always what you’d expect. 

Beyond food or medication allergies, chronic hives can sometimes be linked to:

✨ Autoimmune conditions (like lupus)
✨ Heat or exercise (aka cholinergic urticaria)
✨ Rarely: water, sunlight, or even vibrations 

Sometimes, we never find a clear cause, but knowing the possibilities helps connect the dots. If you’re struggling with ongoing hives, check in with a dermatologist to get the right plan.  #geetayadavmd

Treatment of urticaria
description:
Acute urticaria (less than 6 weeks):
Mild: 2nd gen H1 blocker 
Moderate to severe: H1 + H2 blocker
Severe: add oral steroids

Chronic urticaria (over 6 weeks): 
First line: 
2nd gen H1 blocker for two weeks
Second line, consider: 
Increase dose of H1 blocker (2-4x)
Add 1st gen H1 blocker at bedtime
Add H2 blocker
Add leukotriene receptor antagonist
Brief course of oral steroids
Third line: 
Try another option from above list 
Last-line therapies (hydroxychloroquine, tacrolimus, omalizumab)

title: How to treat Hives (Urticaria)? - Doctor Explains
description:
In this video we will cover how to treat hives, but before we do so I thought it would be important to explain exactly what hives are. 

Hives or urticaria is a kind of skin rash of red, raised, itchy bumps. Which may burn and sting.
Hives can appear anywhere on your skin, affecting a small, pin-point area upwards to an area of several centimeters. 

Hives are caused by an autoimmune reaction, meaning it is caused by a complex cascade of your own immune system, releasing inflammatory mediators and histamine. This results in leakages of fluid from the tiny blood-vessels in your skin causing edema and thereby these hives. 

Therefore hives persist up until this fluid is absorbed into the surrounding cells. In 95% of all cases this takes less than 6 weeks, usually lasting just a few days, leaving no lasting skin-changes. 

In half of the cases the cause is unknown, but many substances in your direct environment might cause hives: an allergic reaction on medication or food, an infection, prolonged pressure on your skin, stress, cold temperatures, contact with water and exercising more than 30 minutes. 

How to treat hives:
- First, you could apply a cool washcloth on your hives to reduce the itching.
- Next take a lukewarm bath or shower. Do not use hot water, don’t soak for more than 10 minutes and don’t use scented lotions, as this can worsens your symptoms. 
- After the shower or bath, pad yourself dry. Don’t scrub or rub your skin. And apply a moisturizer on your damp skin to nourish and calm it .
- Put on 100% cotton clothing and don’t wear wool.
- Also prevent scratching, as this will worsen the rash. Trim your nails and  instead of scratching you could relieve the itching by pressing your skin firmly.
- Lastly if these tips where insufficient your doctor might prescribe you oral antihistamines or oral glucocorticoids. These can help to resolve the hives and reduce the itching. 

If you are looking for more information on these drugs than check out this playlist: 
https://www.youtube.com/playlist?list=PLaG3Bo3L0gX75jvmQBX69RHjf5hsZegCf

- Disclaimer: this video and the comments are meant purely informational! This is not medical advice! If you are looking for medical advice always contact your own doctor. -

Urticaria: Classification, Pathogenesis, Clinical Features, Treatment -
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Introduction to Urticaria -
Urticaria, commonly known as hives, is a skin condition characterized by the sudden appearance of raised, itchy welts that are often red or pale. These lesions are transient, lasting from a few hours to days, and may appear anywhere on the body. Urticaria can be either acute (lasting less than 6 weeks) or chronic (lasting more than 6 weeks). It is caused by histamine release from mast cells and basophils in the skin, leading to localized vasodilation, increased vascular permeability, and edema.

Classification of Urticaria 📊-
Urticaria is classified based on the underlying trigger and duration:
Acute Urticaria:
Lasts less than 6 weeks.
Common causes include infections, food allergies, drug reactions, or insect bites.
Chronic Urticaria:
Persists for more than 6 weeks and is often idiopathic (no identifiable cause).
Can be associated with autoimmune disorders or persistent infections.
Physical Urticaria:
Triggered by physical stimuli, such as:
Dermatographism: Urticaria caused by friction or scratching.
Cold urticaria: Triggered by cold exposure.
Solar urticaria: Triggered by sunlight.
Pressure urticaria: Caused by sustained pressure on the skin (e.g., from tight clothing).
Cholinergic urticaria: Triggered by heat, exercise, or emotional stress.
Contact Urticaria:
Direct contact with substances like latex, plants, or chemicals causes urticaria.
Hereditary Angioedema:
A genetic form of urticaria that includes swelling (angioedema) in deeper layers of the skin.

Pathogenesis of Urticaria 🦠-
The development of urticaria involves the activation of mast cells and basophils, which release histamine and other inflammatory mediators. The key steps include:
Mast cell activation: Triggers can be allergens, physical stimuli, or autoimmune mechanisms.
Histamine release: Leads to vasodilation and increased vascular permeability, resulting in swelling, redness (erythema), and itching.
Edema: Fluid leakage from blood vessels into the surrounding tissue causes the characteristic raised wheals.
Immune response: Urticaria can be mediated by IgE antibodies in allergic reactions, but in chronic cases, it may be driven by autoimmune processes where the body attacks its own IgE receptors on mast cells.

Clinical Features of Urticaria 🧐-
Wheals (Hives): The primary lesion is a raised, red or skin-colored welt that is itchy and typically surrounded by erythema (redness). These wheals can vary in size, from small lesions to large plaques, and they change shape, moving from one area to another.
Pruritus (Itching): Intense itching is a hallmark of urticaria, exacerbated by heat and stress.
Angioedema: In some cases, deeper layers of the skin are affected, leading to angioedema, which presents as swelling, particularly in the face, lips, tongue, hands, feet, and genitals.
Episodic nature: Lesions may come and go over the course of a day, resolving without leaving scars.
Triggers: Episodes may be triggered by allergens (foods, medications), infections, environmental factors, or physical stimuli (e.g., pressure, heat).

Treatment of Urticaria 💊-
Management of urticaria focuses on relieving symptoms and preventing triggers:
Antihistamines:
First-line treatment: H1 antihistamines (e.g., cetirizine, loratadine, diphenhydramine) block histamine receptors and reduce itching and wheal formation.
In chronic or severe cases, H2 antihistamines (e.g., ranitidine) may be added to enhance relief.
Corticosteroids:
Oral corticosteroids (e.g., prednisone) are used for short-term control of severe, acute urticaria but are not recommended for long-term use due to side effects.
Omalizumab:
A monoclonal antibody that blocks IgE, effective in treating chronic urticaria that is resistant to antihistamines.
Epinephrine (Adrenaline):
For anaphylaxis or severe angioedema with breathing difficulty, epinephrine injection is used as an emergency treatment.

title: What is Dermatographism?
description:
Did you know?

Dermatographism is an exaggerated weal and flare response that occurs within minutes of the skin being stroked or scratched. Dermographism is the most common form of physical or chronic inducible urticaria. It is also called dermatographia and dermographic urticaria.

In 25–50% of people, firm stroking of the skin first produces a red flare, then slight swelling along the line of the stroke. In 5% of the population, this response is exaggerated enough to be called dermographism. Only in a minority of cases does it cause symptoms.

title: Females, Pregnancy & Chronic Urticaria: What Doctors Should Know_Episode 108
description:
In this episode, Dr. Sophia Neisinger welcomes Prof. Emek Kocatürk, leading urticaria researcher and UCARE expert from Istanbul, to explore the gender-specific aspects of chronic urticaria, with a special focus on pregnancy. 

🔹 Why do more women than men suffer from chronic urticaria? 
🔹 How does pregnancy affect urticaria symptoms and treatment response? 
🔹 What medications are safe during pregnancy and breastfeeding? 
🔹 How should physicians approach treatment decisions with female patients? 

Prof. Kocatürk shares key insights from the PREG-CU study, involving over 300 pregnant CSU patients, and explains why half of them improve during pregnancy. She outlines practical treatment guidelines for antihistamines and omalizumab use during pregnancy and breastfeeding, discusses estrogen’s inflammatory role, and gives tips for shared decision-making in clinical care. 

Key Learnings from the Episode:

･ 70% of CSU patients are female, pointing to hormonal and autoimmune factors in disease susceptibility. 

･ Female CSU patients suffer more: more angioedema, systemic symptoms, worse disease control, and more comorbidities like asthma, thyroid disease, and depression. 

･ The PREG-CU study showed that 50% of CSU patients improve during pregnancy, offering hope to women planning to conceive. 

･ Exacerbations during pregnancy are common and linked to worse pregnancy outcomes if untreated. 

･ Antihistamines (especially cetirizine, loratadine) are safe in pregnancy; second-generation agents are preferred. 

･ Omalizumab is safe in pregnancy and lactation, particularly starting in the second trimester. 

･ Antihistamines can be safely continued and even up-dosed during breastfeeding. 

･ Keeping CSU under control during and after pregnancy is essential for maternal well-being and newborn bonding. 

･ Shared decision-making and good counseling reduce fear and improve patient experience. 

･ Digital tools, registries like CURE and CARE, and collaborative research are key to improving care for women with urticaria.

title: HIVES, Causes, Signs and Symptoms, Diagnosis and Treatment.
description:
.
Chapters 

0:00 Introduction 
2:04 Causes of Hives
3:26 Symptoms of Hives
3:39 Diagnosis of Hives
4:01 Treatment of Hives


Hives, also known as urticaria, is a kind of skin rash with red, raised, itchy bumps.[1] They may also burn or sting.[2] Often the patches of rash move around.[2] Typically they last a few days and do not leave any long-lasting skin changes.[2] Fewer than 5% of cases last for more than six weeks.[2] The condition frequently recurs.[2]

Hives frequently occur following an infection or as a result of an allergic reaction such as to medication, insect bites, or food.[2] Psychological stress, cold temperature, or vibration may also be a trigger.[1][2] In half of cases the cause remains unknown.[2] Risk factors include having conditions such as hay fever or asthma.[3] Diagnosis is typically based on the appearance.[2] Patch testing may be useful to determine the allergy.[2]

Prevention is by avoiding whatever it is that causes the condition.[2] Treatment is typically with antihistamines such as diphenhydramine and ranitidine.[2] In severe cases, corticosteroids or leukotriene inhibitors may also be used.[2] Keeping the environmental temperature cool is also useful.[2] For cases that last more than six weeks immunosuppressants such as ciclosporin may be used.[2]

About 20% of people are affected.[2] Cases of short duration occur equally in males and females while cases of long duration are more common in females.[4] Cases of short duration are more common among children while cases of long duration are more common among those who are middle aged.[4] Hives have been described at least since the time of Hippocrates.[4] The term urticaria is from the Latin urtica meaning "nettle".[5]

title: Did you find Urticaria or Hives in your Skin??? #ayurveda #ayurvedicmedicine #doctor
chronic urticaria- Top 3 medicine homoeopathy medicine #homoeopathytreatment #urticaria #skincare
Urticaria Symptoms, Treatment, and Causes
description:
Urticaria, commonly known as hives, is a skin condition characterized by raised, itchy welts or bumps on the skin. There are several types of urticaria, each with distinct features:

1. Acute Urticaria: This type of urticaria typically lasts for less than 6 weeks and is often caused by allergies to certain foods, medications, insect stings, or infections. The welts appear suddenly and may disappear within hours or days.

2. Chronic Urticaria: Chronic urticaria persists for more than 6 weeks and can last for months or even years. The cause is often more challenging to identify compared to acute urticaria. Autoimmune reactions, hormonal imbalances, and other underlying health conditions might contribute to this type.

3. Physical Urticaria: This type of urticaria is triggered by physical stimuli like pressure, cold, heat, sunlight, or vibration. Symptoms occur at the site of contact with the trigger and may include itching, redness, and raised welts.

4. Dermatographic Urticaria: Also known as "skin writing" urticaria, this condition is characterized by raised welts that form in response to even minor skin irritation or scratching. The welts resemble the patterns drawn on the skin and usually fade within an hour.

5. Cholinergic Urticaria: Triggered by an increase in body temperature due to sweating, physical exertion, or emotional stress, cholinergic urticaria results in small itchy bumps surrounded by larger red areas.

6. Aquagenic Urticaria: This rare condition involves an allergic reaction to water, resulting in itchy hives when the skin comes into contact with water, regardless of its temperature. The exact cause is not well understood.

7. Solar Urticaria: Exposure to sunlight leads to the development of hives within minutes. It is considered a rare condition and is caused by an abnormal reaction to specific wavelengths of light.

8. Hereditary Angioedema: Although related to urticaria, hereditary angioedema causes deep swelling in the skin and mucous membranes rather than hives. It's a genetic disorder and can be life-threatening if it affects the airway.

It's important to note that the symptoms and triggers of urticaria can vary from person to person. If you suspect you have urticaria, consulting a healthcare professional is recommended for proper diagnosis and management.

So basically what is skin allergy? Skin allergy is nothing but also called as urticaria or hives. So how do the patients comes to us they come to us with multiple red rashes associated with swelling and itching they can also have something called as Angioedema that is nothing but swelling of the lips eyelids and a difficulty in breathing or also pain abdomen so who all can be affected with urticaria or the skin allergy almost everyone can be affected in their life once or twice. So how do they present to us? As I already told you they can have redness swelling and itching and along with the difficulty in breathing. So what can cause skin allergies or the Artic area urticaria can be caused due to any foot intake that we take that can be due to the Chinese items or the colouring items or the flavoring items that has been added to the food or it can be seconded to any kind of infections. Either it can be a urinary tract infection a gastritis even though the dental infections that we usually do not care about that can also cause urticaria and the other infections that can be caused is your eye infections or throat infections or what we usually called as the upper respiratory tract infections or it can also be caused by few of the drugs like usually patients when they take all these painkillers like you know the headache tablets or a painkiller that is to relieve their bone pains that can also cause urticaria. So these were the causes of acute urticaria that is nothing but the skin allergy which lasts less than 6 weeks. So there is another skin allergy which lasts more than 6 weeks which is called as chronic urticaria. So the causes for these can be any kind of physical that is even a cold water or a hot water or even a sun allergy also called as physical urticaria. That is whenever you kind of scratch there is a urticarial rash that comes on your skin called as demographic urticaria. So these are the other urticarias or the skin allergies that patients usually come to us with. So why does this happen why does it happen only to few people and it doesn't happen in everyone it's mainly because of each and every person's individual genetic makeup. So because of it the individual's immunity kind of overreacts with the triggering antigens. So what can a person do about it when he has this kind of skin allergy. So he can use homemade remedies like a little bit of coconut oil or a soothing agent can be added on the skin which kind of calms his skin down or if he's having a lot of rashes then he has to meet a dermatologist where he'll be prescribed few medications like antihistamine that is your levocetrizine, fexofenadine, belastin which are nonsedating antihistamines. If it is very severe that is what we call it as anaphylaxis or a Angioedema where there is swelling of the eyelids, swelling of the lips, a lot of breathing difficulty or a pain abdomen or if he's feeling little bit of uneasiness then he will be put on emergency drugs like intravenous steroids or oral steroids or sometimes even epinine injections has to be given. So when you have these kind of rashes when should you meet a dermatologist or when should you get an investigations done? Usually acute urticaria do not require any kind of investigation. If it is only a chronic urticaria then a doctor or a dermatologist or allergic specialist ask for investigations. So what are the investigations that has to be done it is usually a thyroid profile. It's usually complete blood count with absolute eosinophil count ESR CRP and it is also anti- PPO antibodies which has to be done.

title: Chronic spontaneous urticaria
description:
Etiology / associations: 
80%-90% idiopathic
Atopic disorder
Autoimmune disorders

Signs / symptoms: 
Symptoms for over 6 weeks without apparent trigger
Intensely pruritic, round/serpiginous erythematous plaques
Individual lesions lasting less than 24 hr
± Associated angioedema

Evaluation: 
CBC, CRP or ESR, LFTs, urinalysis
Skin biopsy to exclude urticarial vasculitis or mastocytosis

Treatment / management: 
Initial therapy: 2nd-generation H1 antihistamines
Avoidance of aggravating conditions (eg, heat, NSAIDs)

title: Hives ,, Reducing skin itchiness and redness ,, Histamine hormones 😇.
description:
Hives — also called urticaria (ur-tih-KAR-e-uh) — is a skin reaction that causes itchy welts. Chronic hives are welts that last for more than six weeks and return often over months or years. Often, the cause of chronic hives isn't clear.

The welts often start as itchy patches that turn into swollen welts that vary in size. These welts appear and fade at random as the reaction runs its course.Chronic hives can be very uncomfortable and interfere with sleep and daily activities. For many people, anti-itch medications (antihistamines) provide relief.

Symptoms
Angioedema causes swelling on the face.
AngioedemaOpen pop-up dialog box
Symptoms of chronic hives include:

Batches of welts (wheals) that can arise anywhere on the body
Welts that might be red, purple or skin-colored, depending on your skin color
Welts that vary in size, change shape, and appear and fade repeatedly
Itchiness (pruritus), which can be intense
Painful swelling (angioedema) around the eyes, cheeks or lips
Flares triggered by heat, exercise or stress
Symptoms that persist for more than six weeks and recur often and anytime, sometimes for months or years

Causes
The welts that come with hives are caused by the release of immune system chemicals, such as histamine, into your bloodstream. It's often not known why chronic hives happen or why short-term hives sometimes turn into a long-term problem.

The skin reaction may be triggered by:

Heat or cold
Sunlight
Vibration, such as caused by jogging or using lawnmowers
Pressure on the skin, as from a tight waistband
Medical conditions, such as thyroid disease, infection, allergy and cancer
Complications
Chronic hives don't put you at sudden risk of a serious allergic reaction (anaphylaxis). If you do get hives as part of a severe allergic reaction, seek emergency care. Symptoms of anaphylaxis include dizziness, trouble breathing, and swelling of the tongue, lips, mouth or throat.

Prevention
To lower your likelihood of experiencing hives or angioedema, take the following precautions:

Avoid known triggers. If you know what has triggered your hives, try to avoid that substance.
Bathe and change your clothes. If pollen or animal contact has triggered your hives in the past, take a bath or shower and change your clothes if you're exposed to pollen or animals.

anaphylaxis
mast cell, tissue cell of the immune system of vertebrate animals. Mast cells mediate inflammatory responses such as hypersensitivity and allergic reactions. They are scattered throughout the connective tissues of the body, especially beneath the surface of the skin, near blood vessels and lymphatic vessels, within nerves, throughout the respiratory system, and in the digestive and urinary tracts. Mast cells store a number of different chemical mediators—including histamine, interleukins, proteoglycans (e.g., heparin), and various enzymes—in coarse granules found throughout the cytoplasm of the cell. Upon stimulation by an allergen, the mast cells release the contents of their granules (a process called degranulation) into the surrounding tissues. The chemical mediators produce local responses characteristic of an allergic reaction, such as increased permeability of blood vessels (i.e., inflammation and swelling), contraction of smooth muscles (e.g., bronchial muscles), and increased mucus production.

German medical scientist Paul Ehrlich was the first to describe mast cells, doing so in his doctoral thesis (1878). That mast cells are involved in inflammation and allergic reactions was not realized until the mid-20th century, however, and since that time mast cells have been found to participate in other immune phenomena, including autoimmune disease and innate and adaptive immune responses.

Histamine - a chemical found in some of the body's cells - causes many of the symptoms of allergies, such as a runny nose or sneezing. When a person is allergic to a particular substance, such as a food or dust, the immune system mistakenly believes that this usually harmless substance is actually harmful to the body.

✨ Chronic hives that won’t go away? It could be Chronic Spontaneous Urticaria (CSU)—and Type 2 inflammation may be to blame!  

🔍 CSU isn’t just an allergy—it’s an immune system issue that causes itchy hives & swelling for weeks, months, or even years without a clear trigger. If antihistamines aren’t helping, new treatments may be available to calm your immune system and stop the hives.  

👩‍⚕️ Dr. Payel Gupta explains it all—watch now and learn more at Type2Inflammation.org!  

Special thanks to Sanofi-Regeneron for sponsoring this awareness campaign.

#ChronicHives #CSU #Type2Inflammation #HivesRelief

title: What Causes Urticaria in Children || Skin Rashes and Itching || Ankura Hospital #shorts #urticaria
description:
What Causes Urticaria in Children || Skin Rashes and Itching || Ankura Hospital  
Urticaria, also known as hives, is a common condition in children that causes red, itchy welts to appear on the skin. It can be triggered by various factors, including allergies, infections, certain medications, or environmental triggers. In some cases, stress or temperature changes may also lead to urticaria. Identifying the underlying cause is essential for effective treatment and prevention.

title: Chronic Hives: Symptoms and Diagnosis! #hives #chronichives #urticaria #chronicurticaria
description:
🤔 Is it a rash… or is it chronic hives?

Dr. Bansal breaks it down in this quick explainer:
🔸 Acute hives = less than 6 weeks
🔸 Chronic hives = 6 weeks or longer
🔸 Red, itchy, raised—or even flesh-colored on darker skin tones
🔸 Can show up anywhere… and move around!

Hives (aka urticaria or "welts") can be confusing and frustrating—but recognizing them is the first step to managing them.

Watch the full video and get expert-backed info at chronichives.info 💻👩🏽‍⚕️

title: Hives (urticaria) are  very common
description:
Hives (urticaria) are  very common  and most often caused by  allergies, infections, stress, or medications .                                 
In rare cases,  persistent or unusual hives  can be linked to underlying medical conditions — including blood disorders like lymphoma.
Important reminder 👇

• Most people with hives  do NOT have cancer 

• Don’t self-diagnose or panic

• If hives are  recurrent, unexplained, or not improving , get checked
Seeing a  dermatologist  (and further tests if needed) is the right next step.

Early checks = peace of mind.
Save this for awareness & share responsibly 🤍

description:
Post-COVID urticaria—those persistent, itchy hives—are more than just a skin-deep issue. Emerging evidence suggests that COVID-19 can trigger chronic spontaneous urticaria (CSU), with symptoms lasting well beyond the acute phase of infection. In some cases, these hives persist for over a year, indicating a potential long-term impact on the immune system.

Welcome, viewers, to a crucial discussion surrounding a daily struggle for many - recurring hives. In today's blog, we'll delve into the causes and insights behind daily hives, aiming to shed light on this perplexing condition and provide practical knowledge for those navigating this challenging journey.

Section 1: Understanding Daily Recurring Hives
Hives, or urticaria, are itchy welts on the skin that often appear suddenly. When these hives become a daily recurrence, it adds an extra layer of complexity to the puzzle. Daily recurring hives can be linked to various factors, including allergies, stress, autoimmune conditions, or an underlying medical issue.

Section 2: Causes of Daily Recurring Hives
Identifying the root causes of daily hives is crucial for effective management. Allergic reactions to certain foods, medications, or environmental factors can trigger hives. Stress and anxiety are common contributors, as they can activate the body's immune response, leading to hives. It's essential to explore these potential causes with a healthcare professional for accurate diagnosis.

Section 3: Insights into Daily Hives Triggers
To gain control over daily recurring hives, understanding specific triggers is paramount. Detailed tracking of activities, diet, and environmental exposures can help pinpoint triggers unique to each individual. This insight empowers individuals to make informed lifestyle adjustments that may alleviate or prevent the recurrence of hives.

Section 4: Seeking Professional Guidance
If you're grappling with daily recurring hives, seeking professional medical advice is crucial. Dermatologists and allergists can conduct tests to identify allergic triggers, while immunologists may investigate underlying immune system disorders. Collaborating with healthcare professionals ensures a comprehensive approach to managing and treating daily hives.

Section 5: Practical Strategies for Relief
Managing daily recurring hives often involves a combination of lifestyle adjustments and medical interventions. Antihistamines can help alleviate symptoms, and stress-reducing techniques such as meditation and yoga may provide relief. Identifying and avoiding specific triggers, as determined with professional guidance, is fundamental to long-term management.

Conclusion:
Daily recurring hives can significantly impact one's quality of life, but by understanding the causes and gaining insights into triggers, individuals can take proactive steps toward relief. If you or someone you know is facing this challenge, share this knowledge and encourage seeking professional help for a tailored approach to managing daily hives. Together, let's unravel the enigma of recurring hives and pave the way for a more comfortable and confident life.

Dr. Rasya Dixit |  Phone 📞 : 099018 90588 (Online & in-person appointment can be booked online or by call) | Registration No: 63654 Karnataka Medical Council, 2002
Consultant Dermatologist & Cosmetologist | Dr. Dixit Cosmetic Dermatology Clinic, Bengaluru, India
Hives or Urticaria is very easily diagnosed clinically. When you are doctor sees you they are able to diagnose easily because it is very classical and it does not need a particular examination or test but we have some kind of urticaria which we are calling  urticarial vasculitis which means there is an autoimmune element to the  urticaria where the rashes last for more than 24 hours in any one particular area then we might need to do a skin biopsy to see if there is a blood vessel involvement of the  urticaria. If you ask me what are the dangers of having hives one is if there is something called an angioedema or the swelling of the hands feet face which may then extend into the tongue area causing difficulty in breathing. This is something which is something dangerous when you have hives. Other is called abdominal angioedema where you know there is an allergy in the gastrointestinal tract and is associated vomiting and diarrhea which could lead to electrolyte imbalance. This is another problem with hives so these are the common dangers of hives however let me tell you that most commonly  urticaria is benign. It does not have any of these complications in about 90% of the patients they do not face any of these problems. However if you feel you are getting too much swelling or the rashes are lasting for more than 24 hours or they are associated with severe pain it is time to meet a dermatologist who can actually diagnose these complications of hives using a few test and treat them so that you could get good improvement from your symptom.

description:
Hives, or urticaria, are itchy, red swollen areas that appear suddenly on the skin.
Approximately 20% of the world population experiences hives at least once in their lifetime.
Allergies are the most common cause of hives, including reactions to foods, dust mites, or pollen.
Physical triggers such as heat, cold, or sun exposure can also cause hives.
Hives typically disappear within 24 hours, but episodes lasting more than 6 weeks are considered chronic.
The underlying mechanism of hives involves an allergic reaction and histamine release, causing skin redness and swelling.
Diagnosis of hives is usually based on clinical symptoms.
The main treatment for hives includes avoiding known allergens and taking non-sedating antihistamines.
Angioedema, a more severe form of hives, can become an emergency if it affects the throat and breathing.
For severe cases, especially involving anaphylaxis, immediate medical intervention with epinephrine is crucial.

title: Doctor's Urgent Warning on Blood Pressure Medication & Angioedema Dangerous Side Effects
description:
Did you know that blood pressure medication & angioedema side effects can be life-threatening? I've seen patients develop severely swollen lips and tongues within weeks of starting ACE inhibitors like lisinopril - some didn't survive. Though rare (affecting only 0.1-0.7% of people), this reaction is more common if you're Black, over 65, a smoker, or have allergies. The swelling happens fast and can even block your airway! If your lips or tongue start swelling, don't wait! Seek medical attention IMMEDIATELY. This video isn't about spreading fear about hypertension/high blood pressure meds - it's about arming you with the critical, life-saving knowledge to recognize the warning signs before it's too late. Learn what to look for and what to do immediately.

**Angioedema: Essential Steps You Need to Take Right Away**

Angioedema is a rapid swelling of the deeper layers of the skin, often triggered by allergies, medications, or stress. Here’s what you should do immediately:

1. **Identify the Cause**: Recognize possible triggers like food, medications (e.g., ACE inhibitors), or insect stings.
2. **Administer Antihistamines**: Over-the-counter antihistamines can help control mild cases.
3. **Seek Emergency Help**: If swelling occurs around the throat or face, or breathing becomes difficult, call emergency services immediately.
4. **Follow Up with an Allergist**: To prevent future episodes, consult an allergist for a thorough evaluation and potential treatment plan.

Prompt action is crucial in preventing life-threatening situations like airway obstruction.

title: Idiopathic Angioedema - Case Study #1: The Inflammation Files with Dr. Kara Wada
description:
Case #1 - Itchy Mystery 

Remember Sarah, the young woman struck by sudden, severe hives and swelling?  Yesterday, you played detective, and now it's time for the big reveal...

The answer is B) Idiopathic Angioedema.  But what exactly does that mean?

1️⃣ Swelling That's More Than Skin-Deep: Angioedema causes swelling beneath the skin, which is why Sarah's face was so dramatically affected.

2️⃣ Not Your Average Allergy: Itchy hives are a classic allergy sign, but angioedema often itches less and the swelling is larger and deeper.

3️⃣ The Mystery of "Idiopathic": This means the cause is unknown, adding to the frustration and making diagnosis tricky.

The Good News:  Even though the cause is mysterious, angioedema is usually treatable!  Sarah responded well to medication and is back to enjoying life.

Did this diagnosis surprise you? It highlights how important it is to look beyond the obvious when it comes to allergic-type reactions.

Angioedema is usually triggered by an allergic reaction to foods such as eggs, fish, shellfish, nuts, and fruits.
next venom such as from insect stings or bites, pollen, animal dander, allergy shots, immunotherapy, latex, or medicine.
 An overactive immune system can also cause angioedema. Infections and injuries can also cause it in some people, as can cold heat and exposure to the sun. Angioedema can run in families. This is called hereditary angioedema.

title: Angioedema Explained
description:
Angioedema is a condition characterized by the rapid swelling of the deeper layers of the skin, often occurring in the face, lips, throat, and other areas. This condition is caused by the accumulation of fluid in the deeper layers of the skin, leading to pronounced swelling. Angioedema can be a concerning and potentially life-threatening condition, and understanding its causes, symptoms, and treatment options is crucial for effective management.

Causes of Angioedema:

Angioedema can be classified into two main types: allergic and non-allergic.

Allergic Angioedema:

IgE-Mediated: This type of angioedema is triggered by an immune system response to an allergen. The body releases histamine and other chemicals, leading to swelling.
Non-IgE-Mediated: In some cases, angioedema may occur without involving the typical allergic response mediated by immunoglobulin E (IgE). This can be caused by direct activation of mast cells.
Non-Allergic Angioedema:

Hereditary Angioedema (HAE): This is a rare genetic disorder that leads to the overproduction of bradykinin, a substance that causes blood vessels to leak fluid.
Acquired Angioedema: This type is not inherited but can develop later in life due to various factors, including autoimmune conditions, medications, or infections.
Symptoms of Angioedema:

The primary symptom of angioedema is sudden and pronounced swelling, typically in the following areas:

Face, including the lips and eyes
Hands
Feet
Genitals
Throat
In addition to swelling, individuals with angioedema may experience other symptoms such as redness, warmth, and pain in the affected areas. If the swelling occurs in the throat, it can lead to difficulty breathing, which requires immediate medical attention.

Treatment Options:

Antihistamines: For allergic angioedema, antihistamines are often the first line of defense. They work by blocking the action of histamine, a key player in allergic reactions.

Corticosteroids: In more severe cases, corticosteroids may be prescribed to reduce inflammation and suppress the immune response.

Epinephrine: In cases where angioedema is severe and involves the throat, epinephrine may be administered to quickly alleviate symptoms and improve breathing.

Bradykinin Inhibitors: For hereditary angioedema, medications that target bradykinin, such as icatibant and ecallantide, may be prescribed to prevent and relieve symptoms.

Identifying and Avoiding Triggers: In cases of allergic angioedema, identifying and avoiding the trigger allergen is crucial in preventing future episodes.

Conclusion:

Angioedema is a condition that requires prompt attention, especially when it involves the throat and can lead to respiratory difficulties. Understanding the underlying causes, recognizing the symptoms, and seeking appropriate medical care are essential for managing angioedema effectively. Individuals with a history of angioedema should work closely with healthcare professionals to develop a personalized management plan that addresses their specific needs and triggers.

title: How are urticaria and angioedema treated?
description: The most commonly used treatment for urticaria and angioedema is antihistamines. Antihistamines block the effect of the histamine that is released and reduce the itching and rash in most people. If the urticaria occurs frequently then the antihistamines are best taken regularly as they are much better at preventing attacks than reversing them. Very rarely injections of adrenaline (epinephrine) may be required if there are breathing problems caused by urticaria and  angioedema

title: Episode 974: ACE Inhibitor Angioedema
description:
Contributor: Ricky Dhaliwal, MD

 Educational Pearls:

  Angioedema in anaphylaxis  Histamine and mast cell-mediated pathway Treatment:  First line: epinephrine for vasoconstriction and bronchodilation Second line: H1 and H2 antihistamines such as Benadryl and famotidine     ACE inhibitor-induced angioedema  Different pathway from anaphylaxis  ACE inhibitor-induced angioedema is mediated by bradykinins Therefore, anaphylaxis medications are not beneficial in patients with ACE inhibitor-induced angioedema   Leading cause of drug-induced angioedema in the US Patients most commonly present with swelling of the lips, tongue, or face Treatment:  Airway management: varies depending on the severity and progression of the presentation  If awake nasointubation is required, LMX is a 5% lidocaine water-soluble solution that provides anesthesia to the oropharynx   Medications:  Icatibant is a synthetic bradykinin B2-receptor antagonist that can be used in acute treatment Tranexamic acid (TXA) inhibits the plasmin-dependent formation of bradykinin, but the data on this treatment are mixed and limited Fresh frozen plasma (FFP) is thought to degrade high levels of bradykinin with subsequent resolution of angioedema   Discontinue ACE inhibitor      References

title: Do you give steroids to every angioedema patient?
description:

title: How can one manage Angioedema?-Dr. Aruna Prasad
description: Angioedema is actually an allergic reaction of the skin. So that particular part of the body could be any part of the body becomes reddish, swollen and painful. It could be food, drugs, dust etc. Identify what is the triggering factor and avoidance of that factor will cure angioedema, plus antihistamines. description:
🔍 What is Angioedema, and why should you care? Giant hives can be alarming, but understanding the causes, symptoms, and treatments can empower you to take control. Join us in this comprehensive video as we explore the medical insights behind angioedema and how to effectively manage this condition.


What's Inside This Video 🩺
In this video, we cover:
👉 What is Angioedema? Discover how this condition affects the deeper layers of the skin and its distinction from hives.
👉 Causes of Angioedema: From allergic reactions to genetic predispositions, learn about the triggers behind this condition.
👉 Symptoms and Diagnosis: Recognize the signs, including life-threatening complications, and understand the diagnostic process.
👉 Treatment Options: Explore effective treatments, from antihistamines to emergency interventions like epinephrine.
👉 Management Tips: Learn how to avoid triggers, carry emergency medication, and live a normal, healthy life despite angioedema.

title: What happens in Hives and Angioedema? - Dr. Nischal K
description: Hives and angioedema is nothing but an allergic skin reaction. Its not only seen outside but, it can also involve other internal body parts. Commonly people recognize it is as red itchy lesions which just comes and goes off.  You see for some time and without some time its just gone away without leaving behind any marks. This can be because of two things, one you are reacting to something outside, or you are reacting for something which is internally. So whenever you are reacting internally that is called as autoimmune urticaria, where our immune system is not fighting against something outside, but some protein of its own, the body’s own protein. So that is why it is called as autoimmune. Auto itself  and immune is the immune reaction, the resistance power. When these happens these reactions they go on for a longer duration and it can sometimes cripple an individual. Because of the rashes people do feel very feverish, they can have giddiness, they can have vomiting, their blood pressure can drop down. Not only that, when the swelling involves the nose, the throat, they can have a choking feeling and sometimes it can be very fatal too. Second thing is they could be reacting to outside object. It can be a food allergen sometimes people do react to fish or sea foods, eggs, milk products or wheat. These are food allergens what people react to. Other things are commonly the pollen and this is often seasonal and some people are very prone for that and these individuals are called as atopics. They have a very dry skin. They have a family history of probably a skin allergy, bronchial asthma, that is the wheezing or they have a very itchy eyes or a runny nose or lot of sneezing. These individuals are more prone for this type of a reaction.

title: Most Missed Cases: Angioedema
description:
Follow along as our expert subspecialist reviews this case of angioedema as demonstrated on a computed tomography (CT) imaging study.  Angioedema represents allergy-mediated soft-tissue swelling that can lead to life-threatening airway obstruction. Allergic reactions leading to angioedema can be triggered by certain types of foods, medications, and insect bites and stings.

Over the last 10 years, the Department of Radiology at the University of Florida College of Medicine has conducted a simulation-based evaluation of radiology resident competence in critical care imaging.
 
243 residents interpreted this case of angioedema as one of 65 cases during an 8-hour simulated on-call shift, with a median score of 0 out of 10 and an overall average score of 2.70 out of 10.

On average, 4.35 points out of 10 were lost due to missed observations. At the same time, 2.83 points were lost due to interpretive errors.

We define an effective report to be one which achieves scores between 7 and 10. In terms of letter grades, this would be A & B. In this “most missed” case 22% of residents produced effective reports.

We define a report having a critical error to be one with scores between 0 and 2. In terms of letter grades, this would be F & D. In this “most missed” case 65% of residents produced reports with critical error(s).

View the full DICOM set and final report here: https://bit.ly/3qkFwVt

Claim 0.5 AMA PRA Category 1 CME Credit™ per case by reviewing this case and others available in our Decision Support critical care case library. Use WIDIonline to fill in your knowledge gaps by reviewing our educational curriculum on this subject.  

WIDI Online: https://widionline.xray.ufl.edu/ 
Twitter: https://twitter.com/UFLWIDI 

WIDI SIM is a critical care radiology SIMulation consisting of 65 cases across all subspecialties and modalities, including normals, given over an eight hour “shift” experience.

For more information on WIDI SIM: https://widi.xray.ufl.edu/widi-research/publications/

description:

In this informative video, we discuss acquired angioedema, a condition that causes sudden swelling in various parts of the body, particularly in the skin and mucous membranes. We will explain the differences between acquired angioedema and hereditary angioedema, focusing on how the former develops due to underlying health issues rather than genetic factors. description:

Urticaria, commonly known as hives, presents as itchy, raised welts on the skin, while angioedema involves swelling beneath the skin, often affecting sensitive areas like the eyes and lips. description:
Should we give epi in angioedema?
-Textbook answer is no as angioedema w/ different mechanism to anaphylaxis
-However, some anaphylaxis can have angioedema (more likely if rash present)
-If patient w/ significant airway swelling/compromise, give epi: if anaphylaxis, will rapidly improve patient + if angioedema, won’t harm patient
-If mild swelling, no airway compromise + no other signs of anaphylaxis/allergy, would hold epi

title: Common Causes of Swollen Lips #shorts
description:
Common Causes of Swollen Lips

Full video at https://youtu.be/LX3ktPeypf4

*Education not medical advice

Allergic Reaction: 
Food: Eggs, milk, peanuts, tree nuts, fish, shellfish, wheat, soy
Medications: Penicillin, lisinopril, Stop the medication and contact your healthcare provider
Material: Cosmetics: Stop using
Insect bites

If you have shortness of breath or tightening of the throat call 911 or your local emergency number.

There is no cure for food allergies but you can prevent allergic reactions by avoiding the things you are allergic to.

If you’re having a mild allergic reaction you can take diphenhydramine: Benadryl. 

Oral Allergy Syndrome
Is caused by minor reactions to some raw fruits and vegetables. A contact allergy only affects the part of the mouth the allergen has touched. 
Angioedema: develops due to an allergy such as an allergy to a common medication called lisinopril
You may also develop a raised itchy rash called hives
Pimples near the lip can cause the lip to swell
Skin infection: Cellulitis
Cold sores: herpes virus
Trauma/Injury: Lip biting, getting hit: Put a cold compress on the lip
Infection of the mouth or gums can cause the lips to swell
Sunburn

RARE:

Melkersson-Rosenthal syndrome: that causes swelling in the lips and face, as well as some muscle paralysis.

Treatments:

Stop the offending agent
Apply a cold compress: don’t put ice directly on the lip. This can cause more damage
If it’s a cold sore outbreak use: antiviral cream such as Abreva
For swollen lips caused by inflammation or injury, you can take NSAIDS such as ibuprofen (Advil). Take with food and don’t take if you have uncontrolled high blood pressure or kidney disease (decreased kidney function).

title: Why Is My Tongue Swollen? #shorts #allergies
description:
Is your tongue feeling swollen up? Why would that happen? I'm Dr. Manan Shah, I am an ENT and allergist. Your tongue, like any other tissue in your body, can get swollen. And the most common cause of swelling of your tongue is an allergy. It's usually due to food, something like shellfish or peanuts. And what will happen is your lips or your tongue will get swollen.

If this happens, you have to go see an allergist or if you're having any trouble breathing, you have to go to an emergency room. Basically, when your tongue or your lips swell, if it swells enough, it can swell back in and block off your airway and you can have trouble breathing. If this happens repeatedly, allergy testing can help you identify what's causing it.

And the other cause that I want you guys all to know about is sometimes certain blood pressure medications like lisinopril can cause a thing called angioedema. And the angioedema, again, is swelling of your lips or your tongue. If you'd like to learn more, please subscribe.

title: Clerkship in Pediatrics; Learning to Manage Children Allergies and Anaphylaxis Safely
description:
Managing children with allergies and anaphylaxis safely during your pediatrics clerkship is essential, because these conditions can escalate quickly and require immediate, structured intervention. Here’s a clear guide grounded in current pediatric allergy practice parameters:

🧩 Common Pediatric Allergies
Food allergies: Milk, eggs, peanuts, tree nuts, shellfish.

Drug allergies: Antibiotics (penicillin, sulfa drugs).

Environmental allergies: Dust mites, pollen, animal dander.

Insect stings: Bees, wasps.

⚠️ Recognizing Anaphylaxis
Early recognition is critical. Look for:

Skin/mucosal signs: Hives, swelling of lips/tongue.

Respiratory distress: Wheezing, stridor, difficulty breathing.

Cardiovascular collapse: Dizziness, fainting, hypotension.

Gastrointestinal symptoms: Vomiting, abdominal pain.

🩺 Management Principles
Immediate action

Epinephrine (IM) is the first-line treatment—administer promptly in the thigh.

Call for emergency support and prepare for airway management.

Supportive care

Oxygen, IV fluids, antihistamines, corticosteroids (adjuncts, not substitutes for epinephrine).

Continuous monitoring of vitals.

Post-crisis care

Observe for biphasic reactions (recurrence within hours).

Prescribe epinephrine auto-injectors for future emergencies.

Educate families and schools on recognition and response.

🌟 Clerkship Takeaways
Epinephrine saves lives—never delay its use in suspected anaphylaxis.

Learn to differentiate mild allergic reactions from anaphylaxis.

Always involve families and schools in prevention and emergency planning.

Documentation and clear communication are vital for continuity of care

#Allergy

title: CAUSES OF SWOLLEN LIPS AND HOW TO TREAT
description:
Common Causes of Swollen Lips

Allergic Reaction: 
Food: Eggs, milk, peanuts, tree nuts, fish, shellfish, wheat, soy
Medications: Penicillin, lisinopril, Stop the medication and contact your healthcare provider
Material: Cosmetics: Stop using
Insect bites

If you have shortness of breath or tightening of the throat call 911 or your local emergency number.

There is no cure for food allergies but you can prevent allergic reactions by avoiding the things you are allergic to.

If you’re having a mild allergic reaction you can take diphenhydramine: Benadryl. 

Oral Allergy Syndrome
Is caused by minor reactions to some raw fruits and vegetables. A contact allergy only affects the part of the mouth the allergen has touched. 
Angioedema: develops due to an allergy such as an allergy to a common medication called lisinopril
You may also develop a raised itchy rash called hives
Pimples near the lip can cause the lip to swell
Skin infection: Cellulitis
Cold sores: herpes virus
Trauma/Injury: Lip biting, getting hit: Put a cold compress on the lip
Infection of the mouth or gums can cause the lips to swell
Sunburn

RARE:

Melkersson-Rosenthal syndrome: that causes swelling in the lips and face, as well as some muscle paralysis.

Treatments:

Stop the offending agent
Apply a cold compress: don’t put ice directly on the lip. This can cause more damage
If it’s a cold sore outbreak use: antiviral cream such as Abreva
For swollen lips caused by inflammation or injury, you can take NSAIDS such as ibuprofen (Advil). Take with food and don’t take if you have uncontrolled high blood pressure or kidney disease (decreased kidney function).
